items.title: Airbnb's Incentives
items.textEn: On X, I often come across some version of a stock performance chart showing how Airbnb has performed since its IPO which has been largely flat for more than five years now! It looks particularly unflattering when you compare and contrast to its closest peer: Booking Holdings. Source: KoyFin (MBI Deep Dives readers get 20% discount; just click here ) Generally speaking, these comparisons lead to a barrage of pile on from people complaining about cleaning fees, or pointing out how the originator of “founder mode” has ironically flailing around to drive his own company forward. It appears Chesky himself is acutely aware of this dynamic as he felt the need to respond to one such tweet a couple of days ago. Our stock price went up 5x in the run up to the IPO. This is not an excuse for the last 5 years, but we’ve rebuilt the company from the ground up in that time, and we anticipate better times ahead. The story isn’t over. — Brian Chesky (@bchesky) June 15, 2026 Perhaps one interpretation of Chesky’s tweet is that Airbnb timed the IPO almost perfectly to raise money from the market. However, many were quick to point out that despite Chesky’s message of optimism about Airbnb’s future, he has never ever purchased a single share in the open market and been consistently selling shares since the IPO. If anything, his pace of selling has increased as he sold ~$135 million Airbnb stock in just last three weeks which is more than what he sold in all of 2025. Maybe he’s busy selling Airbnb stocks for funding a new AI company. In any case, such data points may not inspire a lot of confidence for current and prospective shareholders of Airbnb. Source: OpenInsider While these data points can look far from ideal in isolation, it doesn’t quite give you the full picture. I actually think Airbnb has one of the better CEO incentives I have come across while covering 65+ companies over the last six years. One of the reasons Chesky is a repeat seller of the stock is that he receives $1 salary and zero cash bonus from Airbnb. Even though Mark Zuckerberg also receives $1 salary from Meta, the expenses related to cover his personal security lead to cost the company ~$25 million per year. Chesky’s personal security related costs are far more banal and his total compensation from Airbnb was below $300k in each of the last three years. Source: Airbnb Proxy Statement More importantly, Chesky was given a highly ambitious RSU program exactly one month before the IPO which would only fully vest if the stock price became 7x the IPO price. The award was designed in 10 different tranches, and so far only two of the ten tranches have been vested. To the board’s credit, not only did they not revise the ambitious targets down once meeting the stock price targets appears to be increasingly less likely (unfortunately a far more common practice), they also did not award any new options/RSU program for Chesky since the 2020 program. Let me put it this way, if Airbnb stock is at $204 in November 2030 (slightly less than 10% CAGR from today) and Chesky remains CEO of Airbnb then, he will be one of the lowest paying executives in the S&P 500 over the 10-year period. Of course, these RSUs are hardly his primary incentives since he still owns ~$9.3 Billion worth of Airbnb shares. Chesky doesn’t really need any extra incentives for him to want the stock price to go up since he is still the largest shareholder of the company. Source: Airbnb Proxy Statement Perhaps a more relevant question is how Airbnb’s other Named Executive Officers (NEO) are compensated. When I did my Airbnb Deep Dive back in October 2022 , I was slightly put off by the plethora of qualitative metrics to determine management’s annual incentives. Source: Airbnb 2021 Proxy Statement This is what I wrote back then about the annual incentive structure for the rest of the management: “While the metrics the board look at are revelatory to Airbnb's priorities such as direct and unpaid traffic to platform, new hosts and listings etc, there are also quite a few elements in the incentive structure that appears to be subjective in nature. For example, in the "technology" category, the board looks at "improved platform reliability for guests and hosts" in which the management received 15 out of 15. I am not sure what that means and they don't disclose how they are scoring that. Moreover, while this qualitative-heavy metric system gave the management 96% payout in 2021, the board later decided to award them 100% in 2021 anyway because of "the strength of the organization’s performance and acknowledgement that the performance is capped at target for each priority even where attainment exceeded target". That sounds like compensation committee didn't actually do a good job in devising a sound incentive structure.” While the metrics related to annual incentive changed over the years, there were still plenty of qualitative factors even in the 2025 annual incentives. Source: Airbnb 2025 Proxy Statement Thankfully, things are changing in 2026 . I took note of the following in Airbnb’s 2025 proxy statement (emphasis mine): “Beginning in 2026, the people and compensation committee determined to simplify the Bonus Plan structure to focus on key financial metrics that are aligned with Airbnb’s business priorities for the year. Payouts will incentivize revenue growth, which accounts for 75% of the target performance weighting, with the remaining 25% based on Adjusted EBITDA Margin performance .” I am heartened to see the board has eliminated all the qualitative factors which are much easier to game, as evidenced by the fact that management has consistently received full scores in them. For a company that is battling the “mature” label in their core business, revenue growth is the primary weapon to dispel such concern. This incentive system makes it abundantly clear that management too is acutely focused on this metric. The fact that adjusted EBITDA margin still had 25% weight assuages the concern that management won’t necessarily pursue revenue growth with little concern for margin. Of course, you can (rightly) scoff at the idea of “adjusted EBITDA margin” which conveniently adds back stock based compensation. We are looking at a “Silicon Valley” company after all which are increasingly quite “innovative” in financial reporting; so I guess we cannot expect to cure them of all the diseases at once, but Airbnb is certainly on the right track with their incentive tweaks in 2026. Subscribers get the daily journal and five+ years of Deep Dives, i.e. full-length analyses with financial models on 65+ companies. The daily is just how I think out loud between the Deep Dives! Subscribe Current Portfolio Please note that these are NOT my recommendation to buy/sell these securities, but just disclosure from my end so that you can assess potential biases that I may have because of my own personal portfolio holdings. Always consider my write-up my personal investing journal and never forget my objectives, risk tolerance, and constraints may have no resemblance to yours. My current portfolio is disclosed below:
items.summaryZh: 本文分析了Airbnb（ABNB）自上市以来股价表现平平、CEO Brian Chesky的持股与激励机制，以及公司高管的薪酬结构，指出虽然创始人频繁抛售股份，但其薪酬主要来自低工资与巨额RSU（受限股票单位）激励；2026年起公司将奖金计划简化为以收入增长和调整后EBITDA利润率为核心的财务指标，对投资者评估公司治理和未来增长具有参考价值。
items.translationZh:
标题：Airbnb的激励机制

正文：在 X 上，我经常看到某种版本的股票表现图表，显示自 IPO 以来 Airbnb 的表现基本持平，已经平淡了五年多！如果把它与最近的同行 Booking Holdings（Booking集团）进行对比，效果尤为不堪。来源：KoyFin（MBI Deep Dives 读者可享 20% 折扣；点击此处）

一般而言，这类对比会引来一阵猛烈的指责，涉及清洁费的抱怨，或指出“创始人模式”的发起者竟然在自救。Chesky（布赖恩·切斯基）本人显然也意识到这种动态，因为他在几天前回应了一条相关推文。

> 我们的股价在 IPO 前上涨了 5 倍。这并不能为过去 5 年的表现开脱，但我们已经在这段时间里从头重建了公司，预计前景更佳。故事尚未结束。
> — Brian Chesky (@bchesky) 2026年6月15日

或许可以将 Chesky 的推文解读为 Airbnb 把 IPO 的时机把握得几乎完美，以从市场筹集资金。然而，许多人很快指出，尽管 Chesky 对 Airbnb 未来持乐观信息，但他从未在公开市场购买过一股，并且自 IPO 以来一直在持续卖出股票。事实上，他的卖出速度在最近三周内加快，卖出了约 1.35 亿美元的 Airbnb 股票，超过了 2025 年全年卖出的总额。或许他正忙于为一家新 AI 公司筹资。

无论如何，这些数据点单独来看并不足以给现有或潜在股东带来信心。来源：OpenInsider

虽然这些数据点在孤立时看起来并不理想，但它们并未提供完整图景。实际上，我认为在过去六年中覆盖 65+ 公司时，Airbnb 的 CEO 激励机制是我见过的最好的之一。Chesky 之所以频繁卖股，一个原因是他在 Airbnb 的工资只有 1 美元，且没有现金奖金。尽管马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）在 Meta 也领取 1 美元工资，但其个人安保费用每年约为 2500 万美元。相比之下，Chesky 的个人安保费用极为平淡，且过去三年的总薪酬均低于 30 万美元。来源：Airbnb 代理声明（Proxy Statement）

更重要的是，Chesky 在 IPO 前一个月获得了一项高度激进的限制性股票单位（RSU）计划，只有当股价达到 IPO 价的 7 倍时才会全部归属。该奖励分为 10 期，目前仅有两期已经归属。值得赞扬的是，董事会并未在股价目标看似越来越难以实现时下调这些雄心勃勃的目标（不幸的是，这在很多公司更为常见），也未在 2020 年计划之后为 Chesky 再授予任何新期权或 RSU。可以这么说，如果到 2030 年 11 月 Airbnb 股价为 204 美元（比现在年复合增长率约 10% 稍低），且 Chesky 仍然是 CEO，那么在这十年期间他将成为标普 500 指数中报酬最低的高管之一。当然，这些 RSU 并非他的主要激励，因为他仍持有约 93 亿美元的 Airbnb 股份。Chesky 并不需要额外激励来推动股价上涨，因为他已经是公司最大股东。来源：Airbnb 代理声明

也许更相关的问题是 Airbnb 其他高级管理人员（NEO，Named Executive Officers）的薪酬如何。当我在 2022 年 10 月对 Airbnb 进行深度研究时，我对管理层年度激励的众多定性指标略感不安。来源：Airbnb 2021 代理声明

我当时对其余管理层年度激励结构的评论如下：“虽然董事会关注的指标能够揭示 Airbnb 的优先事项，例如平台直接和未付流量、新房东和房源等，但激励结构中也存在大量主观因素。例如，在‘技术’类别中，董事会评估‘提升客人与房东的平台可靠性’，管理层得分为 15 分（满分 15 分）。我不清楚这具体指什么，也未披露评分细节。此外，尽管这种以定性为主的指标体系让管理层在 2021 年获得了 96% 的激励金，董事会随后仍因‘组织绩效的强劲以及已达标的各项优先事项的上限’而在 2021 年将激励金提升至 100%。这听起来像是薪酬委员会在制定合理激励结构方面并未做足功课。”

随着时间推移，年度激励的指标虽有变化，但在 2025 年的激励计划中仍保留了大量定性因素。来源：Airbnb 2025 代理声明

值得庆幸的是，2026 年情况正在改变。我在 Airbnb 2025 代理声明中注意到以下内容（重点为我所加）：“自 2026 年起，人员与薪酬委员会决定简化奖金计划结构，聚焦于与 Airbnb 当年业务重点相匹配的关键财务指标。奖励将以收入增长为主要动力，占目标绩效权重的 75%，剩余 25% 基于调整后 EBITDA（息税折旧摊销前利润）利润率表现。”

我对董事会去除所有易于被操纵的定性因素感到欣慰，因为管理层在这些指标上总是轻易得满分。对于一家在核心业务中被贴上“成熟”标签的公司而言，收入增长是消除此类担忧的核心武器。该激励体系清晰显示管理层也同样关注该指标。调整后 EBITDA 利润率仍占 25% 权重，则缓解了管理层可能仅追求收入增长而忽视利润率的担忧。

当然，你可以（合理地）嘲笑“调整后 EBITDA 利润率”这一概念，因为它便利地将股权激励费用加回。毕竟我们关注的是一家“硅谷”公司，而硅谷公司在财务报告上日益“创新”，所以我们不能指望一次性解决所有问题，但 Airbnb 在 2026 年对激励机制的调整显然是朝正确方向迈进。

订阅者可获得每日简报以及超过五年的深度研究，即对 65+ 公司的全长分析和财务模型。每日简报仅是我在深度研究之间的即时想法！

订阅

当前持仓

请注意，这并非我的买入/卖出建议，仅是为了披露个人持仓，以便您评估可能的偏见。始终将我的写作视为个人投资日志，切勿忘记我的目标、风险承受能力以及约束可能与您完全不同。我的当前持仓如下：

items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Will the MATCH Act Change Chip Controls?
items.textEn: Starting in October 2022, the Biden administration’s Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) updated chip controls like clockwork, with a cadence of updates every year. Since Trump 2.0, however, the clock has stood still. Export controls on semiconductors and semiconductor manufacturing equipment have not been substantively updated since December 2024. The Trump administration has been reticent to update controls and has even considered walking them back. Over the past year alone, there have been tiffs over allowing sales of the H20 and then the H200. But the government has not been united in this stance, and Congress is now taking its own stab at export controls. In April, both the House and the Senate introduced versions of the Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware (MATCH) Act. If passed, the MATCH Act would serve as the legislative branch’s first major attempt to enshrine export control into law, a prerogative that has traditionally been the purview of the executive branch and its agencies. The MATCH Act, which has already passed through the House’s Foreign Affairs Committee (HFAC), would carve export controls on semiconductor-manufacturing equipment (SME) into stone, meaning that America’s posture of chip controls would be almost immutable. This stands in contrast with the status quo of chip controls that can be changed at the whim of an administration. Given the MATCH Act’s potential for near permanence as an enshrined bill, it is worth exploring the bill in detail. This article explores these questions: What does the MATCH Act do? What are the bill’s goals? How likely is the bill to actually accomplish those goals? What are the drawbacks and negative side effects of the MATCH Act? How does the MATCH Act affect geopolitics? I also include some lagniappe at the end where I run the eval of seeing whether Opus 4.8 could’ve done all this analysis itself. What Does the MATCH Act Do? The MATCH has two main goals: (1) cement the core of existing export controls on SME and (2) harmonize allies’ actions with American rules. The MATCH Act compels the executive branch to preserve export controls — both on equipment and against specific actors (certain Chinese companies or facilities) — that are on the books today. These include advanced deposition tools, wafer handlers, EUV and DUV lithography equipment, advanced testers, etchers, and metrology tools — all the usual suspects. The specific actors blacklisted include any Chinese advanced-node facility and the companies SMIC, CXMT, and YMTC. 1 The gravity of this rule is immense: if passed, no future presidential administration could unilaterally turn back the clock on these controls. The most important part of the MATCH Act, though, is in the name: matching Dutch and Japanese export controls to American ones. The bill compels allied nations to adopt and enforce export controls with the same strictness that the U.S. does, something many in Washington feel allies have been slow to do. The critical facet of this is the clause restricting the servicing of existing equipment in covered facilities. Currently, both the Netherlands and Japan allow their companies to service equipment in Chinese fabs, regardless of whether or not that equipment would now be considered prohibited for sale. This is a major gap in export controls. Although American toolmakers like KLA, LAM, and Applied Materials are prohibited from such servicing, ASML and Tokyo Electron are still able to service advanced lithography, deposition, and etching tools in Chinese fabs known to be producing advanced-node semiconductors. The U.S. has previously adopted stricter controls first and given grace to the Netherlands and Japan to synchronize controls in due time; Congress now thinks this grace period is over. If allies do not harmonize their controls with America’s on tools and servicing within 150 days, the bill threatens to unilaterally impose such export controls via the Foreign Direct Product Rule (FDPR) . Because all these tools, from ASML’s lithography machines to TEL etchers, rely on some level of American technology, the U.S. will claim the right to restrict them itself. In theory, if ASML and TEL continue to service these tools in violation of the FDPR, the U.S. reserves the right to cut these companies off from American technology, impose fines, and even seize assets. The House and Senate versions of the MATCH Act differ slightly, with the main difference being a matter of tone. The Senate focuses more on "positive incentives" to ensure diplomatic cooperation with countries like Japan and the Netherlands, whereas the House version seems more willing to force allies’ hands on the matter of export controls. In substance, however, the two versions are identical. Potential Benefits If the U.S. cements its controls and ensures allies similarly adopt and enforce them, it can extend an enduring advanced chip lead over China. By clearly demarcating the line of no return, Congress is establishing the minimum expectation for SME controls. Regardless of whether we sell China advanced chips or bargain with other leverage, the tools to make those advanced chips would officially be off the table for future administrations. The Act’s largest potential for impact comes with restricting tool servicing. Maintenance and service for advanced lithography and etching tools is not an annual checkup but rather an involved and constant process. ASML and TEL maintain full-time staff stationed at fabs to conduct preventative maintenance, tool calibration, part swapping, and more functions that the fab could not operate without. Any time the tool is not running is precious time and money down the drain, and servicing is a crucial part of keeping tools running 24/7. In theory, without the expertise of ASML and TEL engineers to replenish parts and fix tools, China’s most advanced fabs may grind to a halt. Some experts suggest that all these tools would be inoperable within a year. As soon as these tools go down, they will not be up and running again. Fairness is another motivator for passing the MATCH Act. While allied companies reap unfettered revenue (as much as 25% of a company’s income) from servicing tools in China, American companies have watched from the sidelines. We have handcuffed our own companies under the assumption that our allies would follow suit, but our allies have not — at least not all the way. In the interest of national security and commercial interests, we must level the playing field. Potential Drawbacks Good reasons also exist to believe that the MATCH Act will not achieve its mission and may even lead to unintended consequences. Chinese Engineers Can Do the Job In time, China may be able to service equipment themselves and simply resort to domestic alternatives for parts. For current service and maintenance, companies like TEL and ASML have offices across China. Their field service engineers (FSEs) are Chinese engineers, who will not pack their bags and leave even while their companies do. Both TEL and ASML hire candidates directly for work in China, instead of dispatching Japanese or Dutch FSEs to Chinese fabs. Hiring field service engineers as subcontractors, rather than direct employees, is also common practice in the industry, and such subcontractors would inevitably be Chinese companies. 2 If TEL and ASML leave, the engineers who fixed their machines will still be around to fix them, now at a premium salary. Some engineers, particularly at ASML, may be dispatched from the Netherlands or other geographies for certain specialized maintenance, but the reality is that the fabs in China will not screech to a halt due to the MATCH Act. Chinese Companies Can Replace (Some of) the Parts The greatest struggle for China would likely not be maintenance, but rather replenishing parts. Lithography, deposition, and etching tools are constantly requiring part swapping because gases become expended or something breaks down. If TEL and ASML leave, how will these tools receive their parts? My understanding of how this plays out is that no one has an understanding of how it will play out. Take an ASML DUV lithography machine for example. It is the job of at least one ASML engineer to replace an excimer laser when it is exhausted. The company that provides these excimer lasers isn’t ASML, but usually an authorized vendor like ASML-owned Cymer or Japan’s Gigaphoton. If the MATCH Act passes, will Cymer and Gigaphoton still deliver excimer lasers even if ASML isn’t able to touch the machine? The language of the bill is vague enough to leave it up for interpretation. The result would depend on the strength of new Japanese and Dutch controls or on the American executive branch’s enforcement of the bill via the FDPR. Most likely, the administration would defer to the status quo and not try to enforce beyond the bare minimum. Even if Gigaphoton and Cymer cannot touch the machine, mainland Chinese alternatives may throw a wrench into Congress’s plans. A company like Beijing RSLaser could come to replace the machine’s light sources and excimer lasers. Along the same vein, third-party unauthorized vendors for tool parts are rampant in the semiconductor industry. Someone somewhere leaks a part design to a company that wasn’t supposed to have it, and that company can sell the part even if its competitors are prohibited from doing so. Besides maintaining and replacing tools, though, the MATCH Act could accelerate China’s use of indigenous tools. This is more of a concern for etching and deposition tools, as Chinese capabilities are already at or near the frontier required for advanced chip production. Perhaps Chinese machines would have worse throughput, and the country would also struggle to produce enough machines to replace fleets of TEL equipment overnight, but the technical capability is there. Where it is not is lithography machines. If ASML machines were to become inoperable in Chin…
items.summaryZh: 文章介绍了即将立法的 MATCH 法案，其旨在将现有的半导体制造设备（SME）出口控制固化为法律，并要求荷兰和日本等盟友同步美国的严格规则；若通过，将限制美国及其盟友对中国高端芯片设备的销售和服务，对投资者评估芯片产业链和地缘政治风险具有重要意义。
items.translationZh:
标题：MATCH 法案会改变芯片管制吗？

正文：自2022年10月起，拜登政府的工业与安全局（Bureau of Industry and Security，BIS）像钟表一样每年更新一次芯片管制。然而，自特朗普时代以来，时钟却停滞不前。自2024年12月以来，半导体及半导体制造设备的出口管制没有实质性更新。特朗普政府对更新管制持保留态度，甚至考虑撤回。仅在过去一年，就因是否允许销售 H20 及随后 H200 产生争执。但政府内部对此立场并不统一，国会如今正自行尝试制定出口管制。4 月份，众议院和参议院分别推出了《硬件技术管制多边对齐法案》（Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware，MATCH 法案）的版本。如果该法案通过，MATCH 法案将成为立法机构首次将出口管制写入法律的重大尝试，而这 traditionally 是行政部门及其机构的职权范围。已通过众议院外交事务委员会（HFAC）的 MATCH 法案，将把半导体制造设备（SME）的出口管制“刻在石头上”，意味着美国的芯片管制姿态将几乎不可变更。这与当前可以随行政当局意愿随时更改的芯片管制现状形成对比。鉴于 MATCH 法案可能成为永久性法律，值得深入探讨其细节。本文将回答以下问题：MATCH 法案的内容是什么？该法案的目标是什么？它实现目标的可能性有多大？该法案的缺点和负面副作用是什么？它对地缘政治有哪些影响？文末我还会附上一个小实验，评估 Opus 4.8 是否本可以自行完成这些分析。

MATCH 法案的功能是什么？
MATCH 法案有两个主要目标：(1) 巩固现有对 SME 的核心出口管制；(2) 使盟友的行动与美国规则保持一致。该法案强制行政部门保留现行的出口管制——包括对设备的限制以及针对特定主体（某些中国公司或设施）的限制——这些限制已在现行法律中。具体包括先进的沉积工具、晶圆处理机、极紫外（EUV）和深紫外（DUV）光刻设备、先进测试仪、刻蚀机以及计量工具——即通常所指的“嫌疑人”。被列入黑名单的主体包括任何中国先进制程工厂以及公司 SMIC、CXMT 和 YMTC。此规则的分量极大：如果通过，未来的总统行政当局将无法单方面撤销这些管制。MATCH 法案最重要的部分体现在其名称上：使荷兰和日本的出口管制与美国保持同步。该法案要求盟国采用并执行与美国同等严格的出口管制，华盛顿许多人认为盟友的同步进度一直较慢。关键条款是限制对受管制设施的现有设备的服务。目前，荷兰和日本允许其公司在中国晶圆厂服务设备，即使这些设备如今已被视为禁止销售。美国的工具制造商如 KLA、LAM（Lam Research）和 Applied Materials 已被禁止提供此类服务，但 ASML（荷兰）和东京电子（Tokyo Electron）仍可在已知生产先进制程芯片的中国晶圆厂服务先进光刻、沉积和刻蚀设备。美国此前先行实施更严格的管制，并给荷兰和日本一定的宽限期以同步管制；国会现在认为宽限期已结束。如果盟友在 150 天内未将管制与美国对齐，该法案将通过《外国直接产品规则》（Foreign Direct Product Rule，FDPR）单方面实施此类出口管制。由于所有这些工具（从 ASML 的光刻机到 TEL 的刻蚀机）都包含一定程度的美国技术，美国将主张有权自行限制它们。理论上，如果 ASML 和 TEL 继续在违反 FDPR 的情况下提供服务，美国保留切断其美国技术供应、处以罚款甚至没收资产的权利。

众议院和参议院版本的 MATCH 法案略有差异，主要体现在语气上。参议院更侧重于“积极激励”，以确保与日本、荷兰等国的外交合作；而众议院版本则更倾向于强制盟友在出口管制问题上让步。实质上，两版内容基本相同。

潜在收益
如果美国巩固管制并确保盟友同样采用并执行这些管制，它可以在先进芯片领域对中国保持长期领先。通过明确划定不可逆的红线，国会为 SME 管制设定了最低预期。无论是向中国出售先进芯片，还是利用其他杠杆进行谈判，制造这些先进芯片的工具都将正式从未来行政当局的选择中剔除。该法案影响最大的方面是限制工具的维护服务。先进光刻和刻蚀工具的维护与服务并非年度检查，而是持续且复杂的过程。ASML 和 TEL 在晶圆厂内设有全职人员，负责预防性维护、工具校准、部件更换等，若缺少这些服务，晶圆厂将无法正常运转。任何工具停机都意味着宝贵时间和金钱的损失，服务是保持工具 24/7 运行的关键。理论上，如果没有 ASML 和 TEL 工程师提供零件更换和维修，中国最先进的晶圆厂可能会陷入停摆。部分专家认为这些工具在一年内将无法继续运行，一旦停机，就难以重新启动。公平性也是推动 MATCH 法案的动机之一。盟友公司的服务收入（占公司收入的 25% 左右）在中国相当可观，而美国公司则只能旁观。我们曾基于盟友会同步的假设限制了本土公司，但盟友并未全部跟进——至少并未完全同步。出于国家安全和商业利益，我们必须让竞争环境更为公平。

潜在缺点
同样也有充分理由认为 MATCH 法案可能无法实现其目标，甚至会产生意外后果。

中国工程师能够完成工作
随着时间推移，中国或许能够自行服务设备，并转向国产零部件。当前的服务和维护主要由 TEL 和 ASML 在中国设立的办公室完成，其现场服务工程师（FSE）多数为中国工程师，即使公司本身撤出，中国工程师也不会离开。如果 TEL 和 ASML 撤离，这些熟悉设备的工程师仍可继续提供服务，只是薪酬会更高。部分工程师，尤其是 ASML 的，可能会从荷兰或其他地区派遣进行特殊维护，但整体而言，中国的晶圆厂不会因 MATCH 法案而立即停摆。

中国公司可以自行替换（部分）零部件
对中国而言，最大的挑战可能不是维护，而是零部件供应。光刻、沉积和刻蚀工具需要频繁更换零部件，例如气体耗尽或部件损坏。如果 TEL 和 ASML 撤出，这些工具如何获得零部件？以 ASML 的 DUV 光刻机为例，至少需要一名 ASML 工程师更换已耗尽的准分子激光器。提供激光器的并非 ASML 本身，而是其授权供应商，如 ASML 旗下的 Cymer 或日本的 Gigaphoton。如果 MATCH 法案通过，这些供应商是否仍会向中国交付激光器尚不确定，取决于日荷新管制的力度以及美国通过 FDPR 的执法力度。即便官方渠道受阻，中国本土企业如北京 RSLaser 也可能自行研发并替换光源。行业内普遍存在未经授权的零部件供应商，泄露的设计可以让第三方生产并出售该零部件，进而绕过禁令。除了维护和更换零部件，MATCH 法案可能加速中国使用本土工具的进程，特别是在刻蚀和沉积设备方面，中国已有接近前沿的技术。虽然国产机器的产能和吞吐量可能不及外资设备，且短期内难以全面替代 TEL 的整套设备，但技术上是可行的。唯一真正的技术瓶颈仍在光刻机上。如果 ASML 机器在中国变得不可用，中国只能依赖国产光刻技术，短期内可能导致产能下降，但长期来看会推动本土光刻产业的发展。

items.author: https://interconnected.blog/rss/
items.title: Why Do (Some) Chinese AI Labs Distill?
items.textEn: Ever since Anthropic released its report in February on three Chinese AI labs distilling its advanced models to train their own, I have been thinking a lot about adversarial distillation.  I carried my thoughts to China, when I recently visited most of the leading labs there, including the three being accused. I tracked how the issue has evolved quickly into action and legislative proposals in Washington. I have monitored (the situation) of a growing narrative uttered by many that China, the entire country, distills (coded for steals) to make progress in AI, not three labs out of many from China who distills. And with Mythos and Fable coming under the crosshairs of government export control , I’m seeing many confidently predicting that delayed or limited release of American frontier models mean the end of China’s industrial-scale stealing via distillation is near. (You can say, I have been distilling about distillation, hey yo!!) After sitting on this issue for almost four months (I distill slow), here’s what I have learned and what I think is missing or misunderstood in the distillation debate.   Distillation Is Data Strategy There are many legitimate and proper reasons to use distillation in the broader AI landscape, from pure research , to reducing the size of a large model to a smaller one to be deployable on device, to refining a general purpose large model to a more specialized vertical model. In the specific case of adversarial distillation, however, the primary reason why certain labs do it is data . As in to quickly fix a lack of data to improve model capabilities, either generally or for specific use cases, e.g. coding, computer use, etc. If you grasp this dimension, then why those three labs – DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax – were called out by Anthropic, but not other Chinese labs, is not so mysterious. Their relevant commonality is not that they are all from China, but that they are all independent labs struggling to obtain quality data to fuel their progress. Out of the universe of AI labs in China, it is helpful to think of them in two categories: independent labs, like DeepSeek, Moonshot, MiniMax, Z, Stepfun, etc. and labs that live inside a giant tech company, like Qwen in Alibaba, Mimo in Xiaomi, Seed in ByteDance, Hunyuan in Tencent, etc. The independent labs resort to distillation as a shortcut, because they are more desperate for quality data. The labs that live within a big tech umbrella and have access to real world usage data from other business units inside the corporate family can (and do) leverage those data sources. This dynamic is the same as Google’s Gemini models leveraging YouTube data because they all live under the Alphabet umbrella.  Does this mean big tech labs in China don’t practice adversarial distillation? Not necessarily, but at least the Almighty Anthropic has not caught their hands in the cookie jar, or they would have been called out publicly already. But if they do, they don't need to rely on distillation nearly as badly as the independent labs. Any measure to shut off or deter adversarial distillation won’t affect them as much. Thus, data or lack thereof is why some Chinese labs do adversarial distillation, but certainly not all. It is not an industrial-scale, country-wide, anti-imperial socialist undertaking. No Data Advantage in China One myth I want to bust here and now is that China actually does not have a data advantage, when it comes to frontier AI model development.  One thing I picked up on, which others in my group shared during our recent trip there, is that there is no full-fledged data industry in China to feed the AI labs. There is no Scale, Surge, Mercor, Handshake, or any number of data vendors, paying software engineers or management consultants to label and evaluate data, then selling that output to OpenAI or Anthropic for multiple millions of dollars. All this flies in the face of a common view (and misconception) that China has a data advantage because it has a lot of people, they generate a lot of data, and there is no data privacy in China (another misconception), so China has a ton of data for AI. For some verticals, like manufacturing and supply chain, this is true, because there is just a lot of manufacturing and supply chain in China. But when it comes to general purpose knowledge or information that is useful to train large frontier models, much of which is derived from the Internet or structured knowledge work, China has no advantage whatsoever. (Let’s not forget, the Chinese internet also shrinks from time to time.) I distinctly remember a researcher we met telling us how much he loves CommonCrawl , the massive open dataset crawled from the web that every lab used to start their AGI journey. His team later shared that they’ve tried using some data vendors in China, but the quality was poor and couldn’t be guaranteed, so it was a waste of time and limited resources.  So for some labs, resorting to distillation is almost an act of desperation (and probably some laziness too), even if unseemly. Adversarial distillation is a shortcut to harvesting useful data, specifically chain of thought reasoning traces for post-training certain capabilities. Nothing more, nothing less.  Join Interconnected Premium Anti-Distillation As Policy Knowing all this, is it worth making such a big deal out of three (ok may four or five) independent labs’ adversarial distillation? To be clear, Anthropic has every right to deny behaviors that violate its terms of service or simply does not like, much in the same way that my local restaurant can hang up a sign proclaiming its right to refuse service to anyone. As a supporter of free enterprises and free markets, I will always defend Anthropic or any private enterprise’s right to do so.  But if your entire professional and personal identity rests on a hardcore commitment to keep China behind the US in the “ race ” to AGI, I don’t think anti-distillation policy is how you get there. At least, if you think what I shared in this post makes any sense, you should temper your expectations. Lack of a robust data industry is a problem, but not a hard problem. It is not nearly as hard as building fully domestic EUV lithography machines at scale. This problem is also mostly a market dynamic one, not a technical one. If there is demand for a data industry, which is low-margin and low-barrier-to-entry, that demand will get met in China in the same way that American labs’ data hunger is fed.  Does a regulated, delayed, or gated release of Mythos, Fable or more advanced model in the future change the dynamic?  Not really. Because not every lab from China needs the distillation shortcut. If this shortcut is turned off for good, it will be a temporary setback for some labs who have come to rely on this tactic, then life moves on. It will hurt the independent labs the most, less so the big tech labs. Let’s be honest, the hardware usage data from Xiaomi or video data from ByteDance is at least as good as Apple’s or Google’s.  In fact, I shared a thought with Nathan Lambert a while back, when I was still distilling on this issue, that letting some distillation happen may not be a bad idea, if the goal is to keep a competitor behind. After all, contrary to the human experience, the student model (the distilled one) can never be more capable than the teacher model. But since humans are still the ones building AI (recursive self-improvement be damned), the smart, resourceful, and hardworking students do end up more capable than the teacher, whether the teacher keeps teaching or not.
items.summaryZh: 文章指出，中国部分独立AI实验室通过对抗性蒸馏来弥补数据不足，而这并非全行业的普遍做法，也不存在中国在通用前沿模型数据上的优势。作者认为，限制蒸馏不会根本改变中国AI竞争格局，主要影响的是数据匮乏的独立实验室。
items.translationZh:
自从Anthropic在二月份发布报告，指控三家中国AI实验室对其先进模型进行蒸馏以训练自己的模型以来，我一直在思考对抗性蒸馏的问题。当我最近走访了中国大多数领先实验室，包括被指控的三家时，我把这些思考带到了中国。我追踪了这一议题如何迅速演变成华盛顿的立法提案。我也观察到，越来越多的声音在宣称整个中国都在通过蒸馏（被编码为偷窃）来推动AI进步，而不是只有少数实验室在这样做。随着Mythos和Fable被政府出口管制所锁定，我看到很多人自信地预测，延迟或限制美国前沿模型的发布，将意味着中国通过工业规模蒸馏进行的偷窃即将结束。（你可以说，我一直在思考蒸馏，嘿！）在对这一问题思考了将近四个月后（我蒸馏得很慢），以下是我的收获以及我认为在蒸馏争论中缺失或被误解的部分。

蒸馏是一种数据策略
在更广阔的AI领域，使用蒸馏有许多正当且合理的理由，从纯研究、将大型模型压缩到可在设备上部署的更小模型、到将通用大模型细化为更专业的垂直模型。可是，在对抗性蒸馏的特定情形下，实验室之所以采用它的主要原因是数据——即快速弥补数据缺口以提升模型能力，无论是总体还是针对特定用例，如编码、电脑使用等。如果理解了这一点，就不难解释为何Anthropic点名的三家实验室——DeepSeek、Moonshot和MiniMax——会被指责，而不是其他中国实验室。它们的共同点并非全部来自中国，而是都属于独立实验室，极度渴求高质量数据来推动进展。

在中国的AI实验室可以大致分为两类：独立实验室（如DeepSeek、Moonshot、MiniMax、Z、Stepfun等）和隶属于大型科技公司的实验室（如阿里巴巴的Qwen、小米的Mimo、字节跳动的Seed、腾讯的混元等）。独立实验室因为数据更为匮乏，倾向于将蒸馏作为一种捷径；而大型公司内部的实验室可以利用公司内部其他业务单元产生的真实使用数据。例如，Google的Gemini模型能够利用YouTube数据，因为它们同属Alphabet集团。

这是否意味着中国的大型科技实验室不进行对抗性蒸馏？并非一定如此，只是截至目前，Anthropic并未抓到它们的“现行”，否则早已公开点名。不过，即使它们进行蒸馏，也不需要像独立实验室那样依赖蒸馏。因此，任何阻止或遏制对抗性蒸馏的措施对它们的影响相对较小。于是，数据的有无解释了为何部分中国实验室会进行对抗性蒸馏，但这并非全国范围、工业规模的“反帝社会主义”行动。

中国并没有数据优势
我想在此澄清一个误解：在前沿AI模型研发方面，中国并没有数据优势。我们在中国的考察中发现，国内缺乏成熟的数据行业来为AI实验室提供服务。没有Scale、Surge、Mercor、Handshake等数据供应商为OpenAI或Anthropic标注并出售数千万美元的数据。常见的看法是，中国人口众多、数据产生量大且缺乏隐私保护，因而拥有海量AI数据。但这只在制造业、供应链等垂直领域成立，因为中国确实拥有庞大的制造体系。而在通用知识或信息（这些通常来源于互联网或结构化知识工作）方面，中国并不具备优势。（更何况，中国的互联网经常被封闭。）

我清楚记得有位研究员告诉我们，他非常喜欢CommonCrawl——一个从网络抓取的大型开放数据集，几乎所有实验室在开始AGI（通用人工智能）之旅时都会使用。他们后来试过国内的一些数据供应商，但质量差且无法保证，结果是浪费时间和资源。

因此，对一些实验室而言，采用蒸馏几乎是一种迫不得已（甚至带点懒惰）的行为。对抗性蒸馏是一种快速获取有用数据的捷径，尤其是后训练时的思考链推理痕迹，仅此而已。

加入互联的反蒸馏运动——作为政策制定者
了解了上述情况后，是否需要把三（或四、五）家独立实验室的对抗性蒸馏事宜大肆宣扬？显而易见，Anthropic完全有权拒绝违反其服务条款的行为，就像本地餐馆可以挂牌拒绝为任何人服务一样。作为自由企业和自由市场的拥护者，我始终会捍卫Anthropic或任何私营企业的此类权利。

但如果你的职业和个人身份完全建立在“让中国在AGI（通用人工智能）赛跑中落后于美国”的硬核信念上，我不认为反蒸馏政策是实现这一目标的途径。至少，如果你认同本文的观点，就应当调低期望。缺乏成熟的数据产业是一个问题，但并非技术难题——它不像大规模国产EUV（极紫外）光刻机那样困难。这更多是一个市场动态问题：只要对低利润、准入门槛低的数据产业有需求，中国会像美国实验室的需求被满足一样，逐步形成相应供给。

如果未来对Mythos、Fable或更先进的模型实施受监管的、延迟的或受限的发布，这会改变局面吗？并不会。因为并非所有中国实验室都需要蒸馏这一捷径。即便彻底关闭这一途径，也只会对依赖该策略的独立实验室造成暂时性挫折，随后行业会自行适应。对大型科技实验室的影响更小。说实话，小米的硬件使用数据或字节跳动的视频数据，至少跟Apple或Google的同类数据一样有价值。

事实上，我曾与Nathan Lambert讨论过一个想法：如果目标是让竞争者落后，适度允许一些蒸馏也未尝不可。因为与人类经验相反，学生模型（被蒸馏的模型）永远不可能超越教师模型。然而，由于AI仍是由人类构建的（递归自我改进不必然实现），聪明、勤奋的学生最终会变得比教师更强，无论教师是否继续教学。

items.author: https://www.mbi-deepdives.com/feed
items.title: Owning the Hill
items.textEn: Satya Nadella spent part of last weekend writing a think piece on X titled “ A frontier without an ecosystem is not stable ”. I mostly nodded along while reading the piece, but there was also a lot of cynicism from many corners about his prescription of not mortgaging your IP to frontier AI labs as Nadella is clearly talking his book. I do believe Nadella’s posture is defensive, but he’s putting his fingers on the right areas even if he’s making points that are self-serving. What really stood out to me is Nadella’s point about creating a compounding loop that can be largely immune from ever improving frontier model capabilities. From Nadella’s post: Companies need to turn their workflows, domain knowledge, and accumulated judgment into AI systems that improve with each use. Private evals should capture whether a model is actually improving against outcomes that matter to the business (not just external benchmarks!). Private reinforcement learning environments should let models grow stronger on real traces from inside the organization. Its knowledge base makes institutional memory queryable and use of tokens more efficient. This loop becomes the new IP of the firm. I think of it as a hill climbing machine. And unlike most assets, it compounds. Every improved workflow generates better training signal, which accelerates the accumulation of tacit knowledge unique to the firm. The companies that build this early will have an advantage that is hard to replicate, regardless of any new individual model capability. Indeed, a couple of months ago in my piece “ The Pendulum Between Intelligence and Knowledge ”, I highlighted Fin (formerly known as Intercom) which was able to climb the hill in a way Nadella would find gratifying. From Fin’s blog post in March 2026: “As of last week, ~100% of all (English language, chat and email) customer conversations are now running on Apex. Since day 1, the Fin engine has comprised a system of models, and last year we started replacing the off-the-shelf models with our own, custom trained on our proprietary data . But the core answering model was always a frontier labs offering—initially versions of GPT and recently Sonnet 4.0. But now that core answering model is Apex 1.0. This model resolves customer issues at a materially higher rate than any other model available. One of our largest customers in the gaming space saw their resolution rate improve overnight from 68% to 75% (i.e. a reduction in unresolved conversations of 22%). We’ve never seen a jump this large from a single improvement since we started Fin. But importantly it’s also dramatically faster, has fewer hallucinations, and is far cheaper than all other available models—all factors that weigh significantly in the consideration of companies deploying these agents to their service operations .” Source: Fin Blog Perhaps it’s telling that Fin just got acquired by Salesforce yesterday. Speaking of acquisition, SpaceX just decided to acquire Cursor for $60 Billion . Cursor was perhaps one of the first companies to realize the conundrum of not owning the hill. Business Insider yesterday reported that Anthropic initially told Cursor that Claude Code was just a 'research effort'. I suspect this might be a recurring theme from the frontier labs; anytime there is a compelling product market fit that has potentially large addressable market, labs won’t be able to help themselves from building a first-party product to gobble that market. Cursor has been trying to respond to that risk the way Nadella would certainly approve. They built a private benchmark, " CursorBench ," made of real requests from real users, and trained its Composer models with RL against it, grading not just correctness but adherence to a codebase's existing abstractions and software engineering practices. You can see the importance of having such private benchmark below. Please note that the x-axis runs backwards i.e. cost per task falls from $20 on the left to $0 on the right, so the model you want lives in the top-right corner (high score, low cost), not the top-left. Each colored line is a single model dialed across its effort settings, from Low up to Max; as you crank the reasoning effort, the dot climbs but slides left, buying a few more points of score for a lot more tokens, steps, and dollars. The curves are concave, and that concavity is the point: diminishing returns on compute. Fable 5 Medium scores 69.8% at $8.27, while pushing the same model to Max buys 72.9% at $18.02 which is roughly double the spend for three points. Composer 2.5, Cursor's own in-house model, manages 63.2% for just 55 cents and ~15,000 tokens, while Opus 4.8 at full effort barely beats Composer 2.5 but spends ~5x tokens and costs ~14x more for the same task! Source: Cursor Once you have your private eval, the loop can feed itself: a better model ships, serves more users, generates more and better traces and surfaces new failure modes, which expand the eval and the training set, which yields a better model. A recent paper titled “ Test-Time Compute Games ” also drove the point home to me why not having such private eval can be potentially quite damaging for companies running blind as there is a huge incentive misalignment between model providers and their customers. From the paper: “…providers have the flexibility to (dynamically) adjust the amount of test-time compute an LLM uses to respond to a user’s query. However, in a competitive market, this flexibility creates a new strategic dimension beyond how providers price their services. In particular, providers can strategically increase the amount of test-time compute allocated to a user’s query to maximize profit, even if the additional test-time compute contributes little to the quality of the response. Consider a simple illustrative example where, for a given set of queries with verifiable ground truth (e.g., diagnosing patients based on their medical records), two different providers can run their LLMs in either a low-TTC (test time compute) mode (e.g., standard generation) or a high-TTC mode (e.g., chain-of-thought), with average accuracies and generation costs for the providers given by: If both providers price their models with a 25% profit margin over their generation costs, it is easy to see that a user who values each percentage point of accuracy as $0.02 would always select the first provider, who offers them higher value (i.e., $0.02 × accuracy − price) independently of the TTC mode chosen by the second provider. However, to increase their profit, the first provider is financially incentivized to choose the high-TTC mode, even though the low-TTC mode would maximize the sum of the user value and provider profit, and would therefore be socially optimal.” As you can imagine, if you rent intelligence, you cannot inherently control the meter; the counterparty does, and its incentives are structurally misaligned with yours as it profits from selling you a longer, costlier path up the same hill. Without your own evals, you cannot tell whether the compute you’re paying for bought you anything more useful worth paying for. The provider, of course, knows but you’ll be just guessing. Despite this compelling logic, the reality is most tech companies don’t seem to have woken up to this conundrum yet. Perhaps Nadella’s post will finally jolt them out of such inertia and push them to give their best shot at owning their destiny instead of being just another wrapper without any compounding loop. items.summaryZh: Satya Nadella 主张企业应通过私有评估和强化学习，将内部工作流与专有数据转化为自我强化的 AI 系统，形成难以复制的复利竞争优势；文中以 Fin、Salesforce、SpaceX 收购 Cursor 为例，说明拥有自研模型和私有基准可避免被前沿实验室的利润驱动所绑架，对投资者判断科技公司长期竞争力至关重要。
items.translationZh:
标题：拥有高地

正文：Satya Nadella 上周末在 X（前身为 Twitter）上发表了一篇题为《没有生态系统的前沿并不稳定》的思考文章。我在阅读时大多点头同意，但也有不少声音对他不把自己的知识产权（IP）抵押给前沿 AI 实验室的处方持怀疑态度，因为 Nadella 显然在为自己的公司说话。我确实认为 Nadella 的姿态是防御性的，但即使他的观点有自利成分，他仍然指向了正确的方向。最让我印象深刻的是 Nadella 关于创建一个基本上可以免受不断提升的前沿模型能力影响的复利循环的观点。

Nadella 在帖子中写道：公司需要将其工作流、领域知识和累计判断转化为每次使用都能改进的 AI 系统。私有评估应捕捉模型在对业务真正重要的结果上是否真的在改进（而不是仅仅在外部基准上！）。私有强化学习环境应让模型在组织内部的真实轨迹上变得更强大。它的知识库使得机构记忆可查询，令 token 使用更高效。这个循环成为公司的新 IP。我把它想象成一台爬山机器。与大多数资产不同，它会复利。每一次工作流改进都会产生更好的训练信号，加速积累公司特有的隐性知识。早期构建该系统的公司将拥有难以复制的优势，无论任何新的单一模型能力如何提升。

事实上，几个月前我在《智能与知识的钟摆》一文中提到的 Fin（前身为 Intercom）就已经以 Nadella 会欣赏的方式爬上了这座山。Fin 在 2026 年 3 月的博客中写道：“截至上周，几乎 100% 的（英文）聊天和电子邮件客服对话都在使用 Apex。自第一天起，Fin 引擎就由一套模型系统组成，去年我们开始用自有数据定制训练，取代了现成模型。但核心回答模型最初仍是前沿实验室提供的——最早是 GPT 系列，最近是 Sonnet 4.0。现在核心模型已升级为 Apex 1.0。该模型在解决客户问题的成功率上明显高于任何其他可用模型。我们在游戏领域的最大客户之一的解决率一夜之间从 68% 提升到 75%（未解决对话下降 22%）。自 Fin 成立以来，我们从单次改进中从未见过如此大的跳跃。更重要的是，它运行更快、幻觉更少、成本也远低于其他模型——这些因素在企业部署这些代理人时权重极大。”

来源：Fin 博客。或许说明 Fin 刚刚被 Salesforce 收购，这一点值得注意。

说到收购，SpaceX（星际航天）刚决定以 600 亿美元收购 Cursor。Cursor 可能是首批意识到“没有拥有高地”困境的公司之一。Business Insider 报道称 Anthropic（安泰珀）最初向 Cursor 称 Claude Code 只是‘研究项目’。我猜这会成为前沿实验室的常见主题：一旦出现具有潜在大市场的产品‑市场契合度，实验室就会倾向于自行开发一线产品抢占市场。Cursor 正在用 Nadella 会赞同的方式应对这种风险。他们建立了私有基准“CursorBench”，使用真实用户的真实请求，并通过强化学习（RL）在该基准上训练其 Composer 模型，评估不仅包括正确性，还包括对代码库现有抽象和软件工程实践的遵循程度。下面可以看到私有基准的重要性。请注意，横轴是倒序的——成本从左侧的 20 美元下降到右侧的 0 美元，所以你想要的模型位于右上角（高分低成本），而不是左上角。每条彩色曲线代表单一模型在不同算力投入设置下的表现，从低到最高；当你提高推理算力时，得分会上升但成本也会显著增加，曲线呈凹形，这正是关键：算力的边际收益递减。Fable 5 Medium 在 8.27 美元时得分 69.8%，而将同一模型调至最高算力则得分 72.9%，成本翻倍却仅提升 3 分。Cursor 自研的 Composer 2.5 以 0.55 美元、约 15 000 token 的成本实现 63.2% 的得分，而 Opus 4.8 在最高算力下仅略胜一筹，却耗费约 5 倍的 token、14 倍的成本，完成相同任务！

来源：Cursor

一旦拥有私有评估，循环即可自我驱动：更好的模型上线后服务更多用户，产生更多且更优的轨迹数据并暴露新的失效模式，这扩展了评估与训练集，进而产出更好的模型。最近一篇题为《测试时算力博弈》（Test‑Time Compute Games）的论文也让我们明白缺乏私有评估会对企业造成多大危害，因为模型提供商的激励与客户根本不匹配。论文摘录：

“提供商可以动态调整大语言模型在响应用户查询时所使用的测试时算力（Test‑Time Compute，TTC）。然而，在竞争性市场中，这种灵活性创造了超出定价之外的全新战略维度。具体而言，提供商可以有策略地提升单次查询的 TTC，以最大化利润，即便额外的 TTC 对响应质量贡献甚微。设想一个简化情形：针对一组拥有可验证真实答案的查询（例如根据病历诊断患者），两家提供商可以将各自的大模型运行在低 TTC 模式（标准生成）或高 TTC 模式（链式思考），并分别拥有不同的平均准确率和生成成本……如果两家提供商都在生成成本上加收 25% 的利润率，用户若每提升 1% 的准确率价值为 0.02 美元，则会始终选择第一家提供商，因为其在同等成本下提供更高价值。尽管如此，为了提升利润，第一家提供商在财务上被激励选择高 TTC 模式，尽管低 TTC 模式才能同时最大化用户价值和提供商利润，因而更具社会最优性。”

正如你所想，如果你租用智能，就无法自行控制计量表；计量表由对方掌控，其激励结构与你相悖，因为它们从更长、更昂贵的上坡路径中获利。没有自己的评估，你根本无法判断所付算力是否真的为你带来了有价值的提升。提供商当然清楚这一点，而你只能猜测。尽管逻辑如此有说服力，现实是大多数科技公司仍未意识到这一困境。也许 Nadella 的帖子最终会让他们警醒，推动他们争取拥有自己的命运，而不是沦为没有复利循环的包装器。

订阅者可以获得每日简报以及五年以上的深度分析（包括对 65+ 家公司的财务模型）。每日简报仅是我在深度分析之间的思考记录！订阅

当前持仓

请注意，这并非我的买入/卖出建议，仅是披露，以便你评估我可能因个人持仓而产生的偏见。请始终将我的写作视为个人投资日志，记住我的目标、风险承受能力和约束条件可能与你完全不同。我的当前持仓如下：

items.title: 🔌 The Trillion-Dollar Off Switch
items.textEn: Welcome to the Premium edition of How They Make Money . Over 300,000 subscribers turn to us for business and investment insights. In case you missed it: ⚽️ How FIFA Makes Money 🚀 How SpaceX Makes Money 🔎 Why Google is Selling $85B of Itself 📈 Stock Buybacks & Dividends Explained Subscribe now A record IPO, then a shutdown SpaceX just pulled off the largest IPO in history, raising about $75 billion at a valuation near $1.8 trillion . The stock has already surged more than 40% in its first two days, pushing its market cap past $2.5 trillion , nearly as much as Amazon. But it was only the opening act. Anthropic and OpenAI have already filed confidentially to go public, setting the stage for massive IPOs in the coming months. Both could list above $1 trillion . Anthropic’s private valuation has rocketed past OpenAI’s to $965 billion , making the Claude maker, on paper, the most valuable standalone AI lab on earth. But the company just got a reminder of how fragile that value can be. On Friday, Anthropic received a US export-control directive restricting access to its two most powerful models. Because it could not screen users in real time, the company disabled both models worldwide. Every major AI company is choosing where to sit on the build-vs-rent spectrum: Build a frontier model like Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google. Rent one from a company that already has. Until last week, that looked mostly like a trade-off between cost and control. Now there’s another variable: the risk of regulatory shutdown. Today at a glance: The frontier’s very bad week Why building just got riskier Apple’s accidental hedge 1. The frontier's very bad week Read more

标题：🔌 万亿美元的关闭开关

正文：欢迎阅读《他们如何赚钱》Premium 版。超过30万订阅者依赖我们获取商业和投资洞察。若您错过了以下内容：⚽️ FIFA 如何赚钱 🚀 SpaceX 如何赚钱 📈 Google 为什么要出售价值850亿美元的自身股票 回购与分红解析 立即订阅

创纪录的IPO，随后又是停摆
SpaceX 刚刚完成了史上规模最大的 IPO，筹集约750亿美元，估值接近1.8万亿美元。股价在首两天已上涨超过40%，市值突破2.5万亿美元，几乎与亚马逊持平。但这仅是开场戏。Anthropic 和 OpenAI 已经匿名提交上市文件，为未来几个月的大规模 IPO 做准备。两家公司都有望市值超过1万亿美元。Anthropic 的私人估值已超过 OpenAI，达到9650亿美元，使得这家Claude模型的创造者在纸面上成为全球最有价值的独立 AI 实验室。但公司刚刚收到提醒，价值可能多么脆弱。上周五，Anthropic 收到美国出口管制指令，限制其两款最强模型的访问。由于无法实时筛选用户，公司在全球范围内禁用了这两款模型。

每家大型 AI 公司都在决定在“自建‑租用”光谱上的位置：自建前沿模型，如 Anthropic、OpenAI 和 Google；或租用已有公司的模型。直到上周，这主要是成本与控制之间的权衡。现在出现了另一个变量：监管停摆的风险。

items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Emergency Pod: Claude Fable
items.textEn: Last Tuesday, Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 went live as the most powerful model in the world. By Friday it was dark, pulled offline after a private "is-informed" letter from Commerce’s Bureau of Industry and Security restricted foreign access to it, apparently triggered by a jailbreak concern and a phone call from Amazon’s Andy Jassy. An administration that spent two years making fun of the Biden admin for regulatory AI overreach showed they really just caught the AI safety bug. To make sense of it, we’re joined by Chris McGuire , longtime State Department and White House civil servant who worked AI and chips now at CFR. We discuss… The 5:21 PM letter that took the world’s most powerful model offline in a single day Why the ‘let it rip’ admin pivoted to mandatory AI regulation overnight. The overseas-subsidiary loophole, the Sunday emergency patch, and the foundry gap still wide open Listen now on your favorite podcast app . Live Tuesday, Dark by Friday Jordan Schneider: My mayor Muslim My bagel Jewish. Fable is fried. Knicks in five. Congratulations to the New York Knickerbockers and the city of New York on their first championship in 53 years. And a hearty — perhaps — message of concern for the Trump administration’s Bureau of Industry and Security at Commerce, which has been taking care of America’s lead about as well as De’Aaron Fox took care of the basketball over the past two weeks. To discuss why Trump took Fable offline, what’s going on with chip and AI loopholes, and what this all means for the future of AI development in America and beyond, we’re joined today by native New Yorker and longtime State Department and White House civil servant covering AI, chips, and whatnot. You know him, you love him — Chris McGuire. Welcome back to the show. Chris McGuire: I’ve been a Knicks fan much longer than I’ve been any of those things, so that’s the most important thing right now. But yeah — at your urging, had to wear the jersey to celebrate. Won’t be taking it off for at least a week, or until it smells. Jordan Schneider: Just a brief recap. We recorded a ModelTalk episode on Thursday of last week where we were all talking about our Fable impressions — Anthropic’s latest model. Now we have a nice little note when you go to Claude.ai saying Claude Fable 5 is currently unavailable. Why is that? Still sort of unclear, but the facts we know: at some point on Thursday, Andy Jassy gets on the phone with someone in the Trump administration and says, hey, there’s something fishy about this whole Claude model. We found some jailbreaks which can do something. There was some sort of communication between Anthropic and various corners of the Trump administration. And then at 5:21, we get a new export control — and I hand over the reins of this narration to Chris. So what did Commerce put out, and what were the implications? Chris McGuire: Yeah, so there was an is-informed letter — some of the details are still not publicly reported, but basically it was a private letter sent to Anthropic that one way or another regulated the export of the model, the remote access of the model, or the ability to access the model via an API from other countries, or from foreign nationals inside the United States. The Commerce Department has regulated the export of models before, in the Biden administration. The Trump administration actually, interestingly, is not enforcing those particular export controls, but has now introduced these. The authority to regulate foreign persons is called a deemed export control. In the Biden administration we actually generally exempted most of the emerging tech controls from deemed export controls, because of concerns over how it would impact business in the United States. But you can say that a foreign national inside the United States requires a license to receive a good that is controlled for export outside the United States. And then it’s a little unclear how all this fits together in a way that actually regulates API access. There are probably ways you could do it, but without seeing the letter, it’s a little hard to tell. The net result is that it was illegal to export the model, or use the model, by any foreigner — either outside or inside the United States — which obviously made it very hard to serve the model even in the United States, because you’d have to verify who end users were. Presumably they’re actually probably employees at Anthropic who are foreign nationals, who all of a sudden are restricted from accessing the model that they work on. So this obviously put the use of it to a stop. And now it seems like there are discussions going on today to try to resolve this. Jordan Schneider: So first off — is Andy Jassy the Jeremy Sochan of this story? Jeremy Sochan, of course, drafted by the Spurs, worked there for three years, got cut, shows up to the Knicks and wins a championship by spilling all of their internal workings and secrets. Chris McGuire: I don’t know what the motivation is. It’ll be interesting to see, on the Knicks side, how much of a behind-the-scenes role Sochan played in the championship, and how much we had all the intel due to a crafty signing mid-year. Underreported part of the series. Jordan Schneider: Or did the Knicks get access to Mythos during the Glasswing window, use it to hack Wemby’s email, get all the dirt on him in order to whisper disturbing things while he was taking free throws? All right, we’ll stop with this. Sorry. Chris McGuire: Wouldn’t be the first time a sports team hacked another one for competitive advantage. But yeah, I don’t think it happened here. Jordan Schneider: So clearly there’s an overlay of bad blood, or at least no love lost, between this administration and Anthropic. But the more remarkable piece of this story from my perspective: you had this administration talking for two years about how they wanted capitalism to run free, that AI shouldn’t be regulated, that this was important to American development and leadership, and that those safety nicks in the Biden administration were really overreacting. And then you go from that to an executive order which was going to be not all that tight — watered down even more when it came out two weeks ago — to all of a sudden, over the course of apparently 24 hours, deciding that the most powerful model in the world, which is an instantiation of America’s lead over the rest of the world, needs to no longer have access. So, Chris, I’m curious for your reflections on that arc, and maybe what it tells you about the future of AI policymaking. The Sea Change Chris McGuire: The particulars of this individual incident aside, the administration very clearly has fundamentally changed its policy on AI over the last few months. I think the Mythos release really did cause a sea change in Washington on AI policy. The policy has not been "let the private sector rip" for four months or so. I think you saw the culmination of that in the AI executive order, which put forward a voluntary safety regime — but ultimately it’s obviously kind of mandatory. I think this basically shows that it’s mandatory. So it’s logical. This is a real problem, and the administration is realizing that AI regulation, and the very powerful capabilities these models have, requires regulation. To the administration’s credit, they’ve recognized that and pivoted, at least in a broad philosophical sense. They haven’t pivoted all elements of their policy, and they’re also moving in ways that are very reactive and fairly chaotic. What’s clearly needed is a policy that’s not just reactive — where there’s a framework set out of, here’s the expectations, here’s how we regulate things domestically, here’s how we regulate them internationally, here’s what people can and can’t do. And it has a degree of durability. I say a degree, because this is regulating at the frontier and it’s hard. We did this in the Biden administration, and there were degrees to which we’d have to update the controls, and then business would yell at us and say, well, this isn’t predictable. And we’d say, we’re doing our best — we’re trying to create as predictable an environment as we can while responding to national security risks. But we did have some degree of a framework out there. The Trump administration needs to do that. They need to be clear about what is and isn’t allowable domestically. There are apparently some standards in the AI EO for how they’d do model evaluation, but they’re classified — probably helpful if those were public. But regardless, you could work with it. There aren’t that many companies. You also need a corresponding international strategy, of which I think we have none. How are you going to ensure AI infrastructure globally is safe? How are you going to make sure that other countries, when they start making this level of capability — which they will, very soon — are not used against us in the ways the administration is correctly worried that people could inadvertently use current-generation models against us too? There needs to be a comprehensive strategy. We’re just not seeing that. And I feel like that’s a trope in Washington — "we just need a strategy" is what everyone says to anything, so I almost hesitate to say it. But export controls in particular are a powerful, useful tool. A tool that can ensure technology is given to the people you want it given to, and create a kind of international nonproliferation regime to a degree. But instead we have this insane situation where right now Canadians are prevented from accessing these US models, but meanwhile the chips that make the models are free to be sent to China because of loopholes the administration created. So look, I hope the administration recognizes that AI regulation holistically is important, and that this is an indicator export controls are an important and powerful tool…
items.summaryZh: Anthropic 的 Claude Fable 5 在上线后因美国商务部的出口管制信函被迫在一周内下线，凸显了特朗普政府对AI安全监管的快速转向以及当前监管框架的混乱和漏洞，对投资者关注AI监管政策和供应链风险至关重要。
items.translationZh:
标题：Emergency Pod：Claude Fable

正文：上周二，Anthropic（安抚公司）的 Claude Fable 5 以全球最强模型的身份上线。到了周五，它因商务部工业与安全局（Bureau of Industry and Security）发出的私人“已获信息”信函而被下线，信函限制了外国访问，显然是因为模型的越狱（jailbreak）风险以及亚马逊（Amazon）CEO 安迪·贾西（Andy Jassy）的一通电话触发的。此前两年，这个政府一直嘲笑拜登政府对AI的监管过度，现在却真的抓住了AI安全的漏洞。为了解释这一事件，我们邀请了长期在美国国务院和白宫任职、现就职于美国外交关系委员会（CFR）的AI与芯片政策专家克里斯·麦克盖尔（Chris McGuire）。我们讨论了……

- 让世界最强模型在一天内因5点21分的信函而下线的背后原因
- 为什么‘任其发展’的政府在一夜之间转向强制AI监管
- 海外子公司漏洞、周日紧急补丁以及仍然存在的代工（foundry）缺口

请在您喜欢的播客应用上收听。上周二上线，周五下线

Jordan Schneider：我的市长是穆斯林，我的贝果是犹太人。Fable 已经炸了。尼克斯在五场比赛后夺冠。恭喜纽约尼克斯和纽约市，53 年来首次夺得冠军。并且对特朗普政府商务部工业与安全局的关切表达了（或许）深切的关心——该局对美国的领袖保障，就像过去两周德阿龙·福克斯（De’Aaron Fox）对篮球的表现一样糟糕。

我们将讨论特朗普为何让 Fable 下线、芯片与AI监管的漏洞，以及这对美国乃至全球AI发展意味着什么。今天的嘉宾是土生土长的纽约人、长期在国务院和白宫任职、负责AI、芯片等事务的克里斯·麦克盖尔（Chris McGuire）。你认识他，也爱他——克里斯。欢迎回到节目。

Chris McGuire：我做尼克斯球迷的时间比我做以上任何职务的时间都要长，这才是现在最重要的事。但好吧——应你的要求，我得穿上球衣庆祝。至少一周内，甚至更久，我都不会把它脱下来，除非它有味道。

Jordan Schneider：简要回顾。我们上周四录制了 ModelTalk 一期节目，讨论了我们对 Fable 的印象——Anthropic 最新模型。现在当你访问 Claude.ai 时，会看到一条信息，说明 Claude Fable 5 目前不可用。为什么？目前仍不完全清楚，但我们已知的事实如下：在周四的某个时间点，安迪·贾西（Andy Jassy）给特朗普政府的某人打电话，说 Claude 模型有些可疑。我们发现了一些越狱手段可以利用模型。Anthropic 与特朗普政府的多个部门之间有过某种沟通。随后在 5:21，我们收到了新的出口管制……我把叙事的控制权交给克里斯。

Chris McGuire：是的，这是一封已获信息的信——具体细节仍未公开，但基本上是一封私下发给 Anthropic 的信，规定了模型的出口、远程访问或通过 API 供其他国家或美国境内外国人使用的限制。商务部之前在拜登政府时期也对模型实施过出口管制。特朗普政府实际上并未执行那些特定的出口管制，但现在引入了这些限制。对外国人的管控权被称为视同出口管制（deemed export control）。在拜登政府时期，我们通常对大多数新兴技术的视同出口管制予以豁免，担心会影响美国业务。但可以说，居住在美国的外国人若要接收受出口管制的产品，需要获得许可证。这样一来，如何对 API 访问进行监管就变得有些模糊。虽然可能有办法，但如果不见信件原文，很难判断。最终结果是，向任何外国人——无论在美国境外还是境内——出口或使用该模型都是非法的，这显然使得即使在美国内部也难以提供模型服务，因为必须核实最终用户身份。很可能这些受限的是 Anthropic 的员工，他们是外国国籍，突然被限制访问自己所研发的模型。因此，这直接导致了模型的停摆。目前看，正在进行讨论以尝试解决此事。

Jordan Schneider：那么，安迪·贾西是这个故事里的杰里米·索坎（Jeremy Sochan）吗？索坎被马刺选中，效力三年后被裁，转投尼克斯并凭借泄露内部运作和机密夺冠。

Chris McGuire：我不清楚动机是什么。看看尼克斯这边的幕后角色有多少是索坎推动的冠军，以及我们是否因为一次巧妙的中途签约而拥有了情报，这将是一个未被充分报道的细节。

Jordan Schneider：或者尼克斯在玻璃翼（Glasswing）窗口期间访问了 Mythos，黑进了温比（Wemby）的邮箱，获取了他的内部资料，用来在他投篮时进行干扰？好吧，我们就此打住。抱歉。

Chris McGuire：这并非第一次看到体育球队为竞争优势而黑客攻击另一支球队。但我认为这里并未发生这种情况。

Jordan Schneider：显然，这个政府与 Anthropic 之间的关系并不友好。对我而言，这个故事更值得注意的地方在于：该政府两年前曾大肆宣称要让资本自由运行，AI 不应受监管，这对美国的研发和领导地位至关重要，而拜登政府的安全警告则是过度反应。随后却在短短 24 小时内，从一个几乎不紧不慢的行政命令（甚至在两周前被进一步削弱），转变为决定撤销全球最强模型的访问权——这实际上是美国在全球 AI 领先优势的象征。克里斯，我想听听你对这一转变的看法，以及它对未来 AI 政策制定的启示。

Chris McGuire：抛开单个事件的细节不谈，政府在过去几个月里显然根本性地改变了 AI 政策。我认为 Mythos 的发布真的在华盛顿引发了一场海潮（sea change）式的转变。过去四个月，政策已经不再是‘让私营部门任其发展’，我认为你已经在 AI 行政令（executive order）中看到其成果——它提出了自愿的安全制度，但最终仍是强制性的。现在它已经明确为强制。显然这是个真实的问题，政府已认识到 AI 监管以及这些模型的强大能力需要监管。值得肯定的是，他们在宏观哲学层面已转向这一点。虽然他们并未在所有政策细节上转变，而且其行动往往是被动且相当混乱。显然需要的是一个不仅仅是被动反应的政策框架——明确期待、国内监管方式、国际监管方式、以及允许与禁止的行为，并具备一定的持久性。我之所以说‘一定’，是因为这涉及前沿技术，监管非常困难。我们在拜登政府期间也做过类似的事，尽管需要不断更新控制措施，企业会抱怨缺乏可预测性，而我们则尽力在应对国家安全风险的同时，提供尽可能可预测的环境。我们当时确实有一定框架。特朗普政府需要做到这一点。他们必须明确国内哪些行为是允许的，哪些是不允许的。AI 行政令中似乎有关于模型评估的标准，但这些标准被分类保密——如果能公开会更有帮助。无论如何，你可以根据它来操作。公司数量并不多。同时也需要对应的国际战略，而目前我们几乎没有。如何确保全球 AI 基础设施安全？当其他国家也开始制造这类高级模型（他们很快就会实现）时，如何防止这些技术被用于对我们不利的目的？这需要一个全面的战略。我们在华盛顿经常听到‘我们只需要一个战略’，但我几乎不敢轻易下结论。不过，出口管制特别是一个强有力且有用的工具。它可以确保技术交付给我们想要的对象，并在一定程度上建立一种国际防扩散（nonproliferation）机制。但现在的荒唐局面是：加拿大人被阻止访问这些美国模型，然而制造这些模型的芯片却因为政府留下的漏洞而可以自由运往中国。因此，我希望政府认识到整体 AI 监管的重要性，并将此视为出口管制是重要且强大的工具的信号……

items.title: The Geography of Time
items.textEn: Programming Note : As a reminder, every Sunday, I write pieces that are predominantly based on personal experiences which may or may not be loosely connected with investing. If you are reading MBI Deep Dives everyday, I think it would be rather useful for my readers to understand my personal lens a bit better since that presumably affects (at least in some capacity) the way I analyze businesses as well. After three and half years, I went to my home country: Bangladesh with my wife and son Nile. We were concerned how one and half year old Nile would cope with the grueling 20-hour flight from SFO to Dhaka (with six hour transit in Dubai), but thankfully it was uneventful. He was curious and tad bit excited at the first sight of Bangladesh. While I spent my first two and half decades in Bangladesh, it’s been almost a decade since I visited Bangladesh during the summer. Most people living abroad tend to visit the country during winter as the summer heat can be quite punishing. We still decided to go for it because we wanted to celebrate Eid with our family. However, right after landing, the humid, musty Bangladesh summer got us all drenched in sweat while rummaging for our luggage. I wasn’t bothered by the heat; if anything, I was almost nostalgic. The scent of hot, humid Bangladesh summer can be unpleasant to the unfamiliar but to someone who grew up there, it was a return to the familiar milieu that got lost for a decade. I was a little worried how Nile would handle the heat, but even he appeared to be nonchalant about it and was busy watching people. For the uninitiated, Bangladesh is the size of the New York state but has half the population of the US. So, if you are into peoplewatching, Bangladesh has plenty to offer! As we headed from the airport to my brother’s place, I was pleasantly surprised by the elevated expressways which likely cut down the time to our destination by at least half (if not more). I often joke with my friends that perhaps half the people who leave Bangladesh decide to do so while being stuck in traffic as they wonder there must be more to life than spending two-three hours everyday in traffic. I only learned to drive after moving to the US as we never had a car growing up in Bangladesh. But now that I know how to drive, it was quite a nerve wracking experience just sitting in the car in Bangladesh. There is traffic everywhere and hardly anyone is following any traffic rule. Frankly speaking, I felt if you are commuting in Bangladesh, you should consider everyday a little miracle that you made it back in one piece. After spending just one night in Dhaka, we traveled the next day to Bogura , the small city in Northern Bangladesh where both my wife and I grew up. Nile received quite the reception from both sets of grandparents. Thankfully, since both grandparents’ place is literally two-minute walking distance from each other, Nile was busy hopping from one grandparent to another everyday. My romanticism about Bangladesh started to fade the more time I spent in Bogura. The reality is even though I migrated to the Northern Hemisphere back in 2017, it was a long road of struggle to make it as a first generation of immigrant. But as things started to fall into places in the last couple of years, I discovered that I have become surprisingly used to certain amenities in life. It was almost impossible to maintain my daily 10k steps habit as the summer heat and dusty air reminded me that I was giving myself too much credit for being able to build a good habit. Perhaps California weather is simply nice enough to make it easier to stick to such habit. Moreover, as I was gaining weight after consuming copious amount of unhealthy Bangladeshi food every meal, I started googling about GLP-1. The worst was, of course, that my sleep schedule got completely destroyed and by the time I could get back to some normalcy, I had to hop onto my return flight to California. It also didn’t help that I probably wasn’t in the greatest frame of mind while in Bangladesh. I was in the middle of two big work related stressors after discovering the myriad tax related complexities for owning a company incorporated in Canada but living in the US. I decided to move my business from Canada to the US which forced me to migrate all the subscriptions from Stripe Canada to Stripe US. This proved to be significantly more challenging than I expected going in and I was coordinating among Ghost, Substack, Stripe, and a developer I hired for the project. I know the payment stocks are getting killed in the market, but the whole experience reminded me that the last thing I want to change again is my payments infrastructure. Anyways, I was particularly curious about how people in Bangladesh are using AI. The fact that AI is a big deal is not news to anyone I interacted in Bangladesh. Everyone is familiar with ChatGPT, but Gemini was also frequently mentioned. While almost everyone uses the free versions, there are a handful of people who mentioned they pay for the subscription. However, they don’t buy the subscription directly from ChatGPT. Apparently, there are some “Facebook Groups” which sell ChatGPT subscriptions. They will give you a specific email address and password with access to ChatGPT subscription for ~$3-5/month. Presumably, if they give the same email address to ten people, you can see these resellers are making handsome margin (ironically almost certainly far higher than OpenAI itself). Of course, the main downside for such “subscribers” is they have no privacy as their queries can be seen by ten other people who have the access to the same account. If large part of the world needs to make a trade-off between privacy and intelligence, it’s not particularly a difficult choice for many people. However, almost everyone I talked to seems to be still confined to the “chatbot” era of AI. I haven’t heard anyone mentioning “agent” once while discussing AI in Bangladesh. Speaking of Facebook, I came across another story from a friend that is less than ideal for…Meta. My friend ordered some fish via a Facebook page. The payment went through but the products were never delivered. When he dug into see why he fell for a scam, it turns out someone cloned the Facebook page he was looking for and the scammer even managed to get thousands of likes (almost similar to the original page) presumably by promoting ads (or who knows maybe via “click farms”). What surprised my friend was that he actually spoke with the seller over the phone and confirmed the order only after speaking directly to the seller. You probably guessed where it’s going…the scammer used AI to clone the voice of the original seller! You can only imagine how much money will be lost to such sophisticated scams in the age of AI. Unfortunately, AI must be a godsend to the scammers all over the world and it’s going to be a long, difficult fight among the platforms, scammers, and users. Even in the age of AI, there are some things that never seem to change. I visited my paternal grandparents village in Bogura. I still marvel thinking about the fact that my paternal grandparent was born in 1920 and died in 2000 in the same village and yet the very land beneath his feet belonged to three different nations in his lifetime. He was born at the time the British still ruled the subcontinent, then the village was part of Pakistan after the British left in 1947, and then it changed again to be part of Bangladesh when the country became independent in 1971. Whenever I want to imagine how dynamic the world around you can be, I just remind this personal anecdote to me. Anyways, this village got access to electricity only a decade ago and every time I go there, I am struck by how unevenly distributed time really is. If California represents the vanguard of 2026, walking through this village feels like stepping through a portal into a different century entirely. I captured the below with my Meta Glasses while strolling through the village: 0:00 / 0:38 1× Our summer trip to Bangladesh was sweaty, and logistically chaotic, but seeing Nile bounce between his grandparents made the expensive 20-hour flight worth it. The romanticism of my youth might have faded into the practical realities of adulthood, but the roots remain. As we settled back into our routine under the beautiful California sun, I realized time moves differently depending on where you stand and I’m grateful for the chance to have stood in both places this summer. items.summaryZh: 作者分享了回孟加拉国探亲的个人经历，描述了当地炎热、交通混乱以及AI技术在当地的使用情况，提醒投资者在不同地区的生活节奏和技术渗透差异可能影响消费与支付行业的风险。
items.translationZh: 编程提示：作为提醒，我每周日都会写一些主要基于个人经历的文章，这些经历可能与投资有或没有松散的联系。如果你每天都在阅读MBI深度分析，我认为让读者更好地理解我的个人视角会很有用，因为这显然（至少在某种程度上）影响了我分析业务的方式。三年半后，我和妻子以及一岁半的儿子Nile回到了我的祖国孟加拉国。我们担心Nile如何应对从旧金山(SFO)到达卡的艰苦20小时航班（在迪拜中转六小时），但所幸一切顺利。他对孟加拉国的第一眼充满好奇和一点点兴奋。虽然我在孟加拉国度过了前二十多年，但自从夏季以来，我已经近十年未曾回访。大多数在海外生活的人倾向于在冬季回国，因为夏季的炎热相当严酷。我们仍然决定前往，是因为想和家人一起庆祝开斋节。然而，一降落，潮湿、闷热的孟加拉国夏天就把我们全都浸透了汗水，同时在寻找行李。对我来说热并不困扰，倒是让我几乎产生了怀旧之情。对不熟悉的人来说，潮湿闷热的孟加拉夏季气味可能令人不快，但对在此长大的我而言，这是一种熟悉的氛围，十年来失去的记忆重新回到了眼前。我有点担心Nile会怎样应对高温，但他显得毫不在意，只是忙着看人。对外行人而言，孟加拉国的面积相当于纽约州，但人口只有美国的一半。因此，如果你喜欢观察人群，孟加拉国有很多可以看！从机场前往我兄长家的路上，我对新建的高架道路感到惊喜，这大大缩短了我们到达目的地的时间，至少减半甚至更多。我常和朋友开玩笑说，也许有一半离开孟加拉国的人是因为在交通堵塞中苦恼，觉得生活还有别的可能。我是在搬到美国后才学会开车的，因为在孟加拉国成长时我们从未拥有汽车。但如今会开车后，在孟加拉开车的经历却相当让人紧张。到处都是交通，几乎没有人遵守交通规则。老实说，我觉得如果你在孟加拉通勤，每天都算是个小奇迹，能够安然无恙地回到家。我们在达卡只住了一晚，第二天就前往博古拉——我和妻子在北孟加拉长大的小城。Nile受到祖父母们热烈的欢迎。幸好两位祖父母的住所相距仅两分钟步行，Nile每天都在两个祖父母家之间蹦来蹦去。随着我在博古拉逗留的时间增加，我对孟加拉的浪漫情怀逐渐消退。现实是，虽然我在2017年迁居北半球，作为第一代移民的路程一直很艰难。但随着近几年情况逐步好转，我发现自己竟然对生活中的某些便利产生了惊人的依赖。夏季的高温和尘土让保持每日1万步的习惯几乎不可能，提醒我对自己过于自信，认为能够养成好习惯。也许加州的气候本身就更有利于坚持此类习惯。此外，随着我因每餐大量食用不健康的孟加拉美食而体重增加，我开始在谷歌上搜索GLP-1（胰高血糖素样肽‑1）。更糟的是，我的作息被彻底打乱，等到恢复正常时，我已经得赶回加州的返程航班。而且在孟加拉期间我的心境也并不最佳——我正面临两件大的工作压力，一是发现拥有一家在加拿大注册、但居住在美国的公司在税务上极为复杂，二是决定将业务从加拿大迁至美国，这迫使我把所有订阅服务从Stripe加拿大迁移到Stripe美国。这比我预想的要困难得多，我需要在Ghost、Substack、Stripe以及雇佣的开发者之间进行协调。我知道支付类股票在市场上正受到打压，但整个经历让我再次确认，我最不想再变动的就是我的支付基础设施。总之，我特别好奇孟加拉人是如何使用人工智能的。AI是一个大事，这在我在孟加拉认识的任何人那里都不是什么新鲜事。大家都熟悉ChatGPT，但Gemini（谷歌的AI模型）也经常被提及。虽然几乎所有人都使用免费版，但也有少数人提到他们为订阅付费。但他们并非直接向ChatGPT购买订阅。据说，有一些“Facebook群组”会转售ChatGPT订阅。他们会提供一个特定的邮箱和密码，供用户以约每月3‑5美元的价格使用ChatGPT订阅。如果同一个邮箱被十个人使用，这些转售商的利润空间相当可观（讽刺地说，这几乎高于OpenAI本身）。当然，这类“订阅者”的主要缺点是隐私缺失，因为他们的查询会被其他使用同一账户的人看到。如果全球很多人需要在隐私与智能之间做权衡，这对多数人来说并不是特别困难的选择。然而，几乎所有我交谈的人仍然停留在AI的“聊天机器人”时代，我没有听到任何人提到“代理”（agent）这个概念。说到Facebook，我还听到一个不太理想的关于Meta（脸书）的故事。我朋友通过一个Facebook页面订购了鱼，付款成功但商品从未送达。当他追查原因时，发现对方克隆了他原本想购买的Facebook页面，且该骗子页面竟也获得了数千个点赞（几乎与原页面相似），很可能是通过投放广告（或使用点击农场）实现的。更让我朋友惊讶的是，他实际上已经通过电话与卖家通话并确认订单后才完成交易。你大概已经猜到结局了……骗子使用AI克隆了原卖家的声音！可以想象，在AI时代，这类高度精密的诈骗会导致巨额损失。遗憾的是，AI对全球诈骗者而言是一种福音，这场旷日持久的斗争将持续在平台、诈骗者和用户之间展开。即便在AI时代，有些事似乎永不改变。我参观了位于博古拉的父亲祖父母的乡村。我仍对这样一个事实感到惊叹：我的祖父于1920年出生，2000年在同一村子去世，而他脚下的土地在他的一生中归属了三个不同的国家。他出生时仍是英国殖民时期，1947年英属印度分治后该地区成为巴基斯坦的一部分，1971年独立后又成为孟加拉国。每当我想象世界的多变性，我就会想起这个个人轶事。该村在十年前才通电，每次回去我都被时间分配的极度不均所震撼。如果说加州是2026年的前沿，那么走进这个村庄就像踏入了另一个世纪的门户。我用Meta（脸书）眼镜记录了以下画面：0:00 / 0:38 我们的孟加拉夏季旅行汗流浃背且后勤混乱，但看着Nile在祖父母间蹦跳，使得昂贵的20小时飞行变得值得。青春的浪漫或许已被成年后的现实冲淡，但根基依然在。我在加州的温暖阳光下恢复日常时，意识到时间的流逝取决于站在何处，我感激今年夏天能在两地皆有所体验。订阅者将获得每日日志以及五年以上的深度分析，包括对65+家公司的完整财务模型。每日日志只是我在深度分析之间的随想！订阅 当前投资组合 请注意，这并不是对这些证券的买卖推荐，只是我的披露，以便你评估因我的个人投资组合可能产生的偏见。始终将我的写作视为个人投资日志，切记我的目标、风险承受能力和约束条件可能与你完全不同。以下是我的当前投资组合披露：
